action_tracker.basis: AI can suggest actions, but BETA only treats metrics, imported inputs, proof reports, and work logs as evidence.
action_tracker.summary: Actions are built from deterministic BETA guidance, manager risks, measurement gaps, and advisory AI output. Statuses are gated by real evidence availability.

data_authenticity.policy: Only accepted real reports are used for metrics, graphs, claims, progress, and AI analysis. Synthetic/demo/local proof reports are quarantined.

data_lineage.comparison_method: The latest proof report is compared with the previous proof report that has the same scenario id, duration, and step size.
data_lineage.data_policy: Only accepted real reports are used for metrics, graphs, claims, progress, and AI analysis. Synthetic/demo/local proof reports are quarantined.

ai.truth_policy: AI was not used. Metrics and claims came from the deterministic BETA engine.

data_provenance.data_policy: Only accepted real reports are used for metrics, graphs, claims, progress, and AI analysis. Synthetic/demo/local proof reports are quarantined.

checks.detail: Accepted real reports: 0; quarantined demo/synthetic reports: 0.
checks.label: Real proof report is available

checks.detail: 0 report(s) were excluded from metrics before scoring.
checks.label: Synthetic/demo reports are quarantined

gaps: No accepted real proof reports are available for metrics, progress, claims, or AI analysis.
Run or import a real bench, field, CI, or project proof report that does not contain demo/synthetic/local-proof markers.
data_quality.label: needs-real-data

data_sources.label: Real Proof Reports
data_sources.meaning: Only accepted real run reports drive charts, regressions, findings, claims, and AI analysis.
data_sources.next_step: Import real bench, field, CI, hardware, or project proof reports with no demo/synthetic/local-proof markers.

data_sources.label: Comparable Baseline
data_sources.meaning: A matching prior scenario separates real progress from one-off numbers.
data_sources.next_step: Repeat the same scenario after changes so every metric has a before/after comparison.

data_sources.label: Repeatability
data_sources.meaning: Multiple runs expose noise, flaky behavior, and repeated regressions.
data_sources.next_step: Run the same scenario several times before treating a change as proven.

data_sources.label: Scenario Diversity
data_sources.meaning: More scenarios reduce the risk of proving only one narrow demo path.
data_sources.next_step: Add at least one scale or field-like scenario beside the current coastal proof.

data_sources.label: Sample Scale
data_sources.meaning: Larger samples make performance, storage, and reliability claims harder to fake.
data_sources.next_step: Run proof scenarios at 100, 500, and 1000+ observations and compare the curves.

data_sources.label: AI Analyst
data_sources.meaning: Ollama reviews weak evidence, missing measurements, experiment ideas, and next actions.
data_sources.next_step: Use auto-strong for serious reviews; keep deterministic metrics as the source of truth.

data_sources.label: Project Scan
data_sources.meaning: Project profiles connect source, docs, tests, firmware, evidence, and planned metrics.
data_sources.next_step: Run plan-ai after major repo or hardware-documentation changes.

data_sources.label: Local Repo Snapshots
data_sources.meaning: Repo snapshots capture Git state, file mix, test/doc/config signals, and local structure for project planning.
data_sources.next_step: Use Collect Project Snapshot on each project after important work or before a planning review.

data_sources.label: Source Connection Plan
data_sources.meaning: Source connectors define which project inputs BETA should expect for issues, CI, docs, bench, field, metrics, and AI sessions.
data_sources.next_step: Register planned sources, then connect real files or folders as they become available.

data_sources.label: Field And Bench Logs
data_sources.meaning: Power, thermal, calibration, endurance, and human acceptance data prove real-world readiness.
data_sources.next_step: Create a simple CSV or report.json path for bench and field validation evidence.

data_sources.label: Project Todo Ledger
data_sources.meaning: Project todos show planned work, active focus, blockers, due dates, and completion evidence.
data_sources.next_step: Add todos for the next project-management cycle, then mark work doing, blocked, done, or dropped as reality changes.

data_sources.label: Work Session Ledger
data_sources.meaning: Structured work sessions explain where time went, what produced evidence, and which blockers or rework consumed effort.
data_sources.next_step: Record work sessions after meaningful project work with category, minutes, outcome, evidence, blockers, and next step.

data_sources.label: Issue And CI History
data_sources.meaning: Project-management evidence needs defects, milestones, build pass rate, coverage, and flaky-test history.
data_sources.next_step: Add a connector/importer for issues, milestones, CI status, coverage, and release notes.

data_sources.label: AI Work Sessions
data_sources.meaning: AI session summaries show what an AI helped change, suggested, tested, or left uncertain.
data_sources.next_step: Import Codex/ChatGPT/session summaries as source_type=ai-session after meaningful project work.

capture_steps.detail: Generate a non-imported template so the team can see the expected report shape.

capture_steps.command: Do not mark the report real until values came from an actual measured run.
capture_steps.detail: Run a real bench, field, CI, firmware, hardware, or operator check and keep the notes/logs.
Use record-evidence with the measured values so BETA writes report.json in the project evidence folder.
A second matching real run is what turns current-state evidence into improvement evidence.

metric_fields.success_evidence: Pass rate holds at the project target and regressions are linked to specific work.
metric_fields.title: Software Test Pass Rate
metric_fields.why: Proves BETA is still correct while project-management and AI features are added.

metric_fields.success_evidence: Coverage holds above the project floor and does not regress against the last import.
metric_fields.title: Code Coverage
metric_fields.why: Coverage shows how much of the code the passing tests actually exercise, so a green build is not hiding untested paths.

metric_fields.success_evidence: Test failures reach zero and any new failure is linked to a specific change.
metric_fields.title: Open Test Failures
metric_fields.why: Failing tests point directly at what is broken right now and block any honest claim of done.

metric_fields.success_evidence: Open blocker count trends down or each blocker has a named next action.

metric_fields.why: Open blockers explain what is behind schedule and what the project manager AI should focus on.

metric_fields.success_evidence: High-value AI recommendations have a decision, owner, and result metric.
metric_fields.title: AI Recommendation Follow-Through
metric_fields.why: AI advice should become tracked actions whose impact can be measured later.

metric_fields.success_evidence: Failure rate and stale issue load trend down over repeated project cycles.
metric_fields.title: Issue And CI History
metric_fields.why: Build failures, flaky tests, and unresolved issues are real workflow health signals.

quality_rules: Templates, drafts, local-proof reports, and demo scenarios remain quarantined.
Use the same scenario id when you want BETA to judge progress against a baseline.
Record failed real runs too; failure history is useful project-management evidence.
Do not use AI advice as proof unless a real report, CI result, field note, or bench measurement supports it.
Firmware runs should include serial frame or operator evidence proving physical identity, board tag, MAC, temperature/battery health, and OLED state.

required_fields.why: Lets BETA accept the report as usable evidence instead of quarantining it.

required_fields.value: bench | field | ci | firmware | hardware | operator | measured
required_fields.why: Tells BETA what kind of real-world source produced the measurement.
Matching scenario ids allow before/after comparisons over time.
Correctness gates are the minimum proof before performance claims matter.
Sample size affects confidence in throughput, storage, and latency claims.
Keeps the metric tied to the actual test, bench note, CI run, or field observation.
Lets a reviewer trace the metric back to the file, capture, or note that produced it.

starter_scenarios.done_when: One measured report imports as real, required gates pass, and the dashboard shows one real run.
starter_scenarios.title: Bench Validation Baseline
starter_scenarios.why: Creates the first accepted real baseline so charts and claims stop being empty.

starter_scenarios.done_when: Portal payload and query p95 are recorded from a constrained or remote path.
starter_scenarios.title: Field-Link Load Check
starter_scenarios.why: Connects portal/API responsiveness to real network conditions instead of local-only timing.

starter_scenarios.done_when: CI result evidence is attached and repeat failures trend down over time.
starter_scenarios.title: CI Regression Evidence
starter_scenarios.why: Adds build/test pass history so project progress is not inferred only from proof runs.

evidence_capture.summary: CosNFX needs its first accepted real report before metrics can prove progress.

groups.planning_use: Do this first when real_count is zero or reports are quarantined.
groups.purpose: Separates real evidence from templates, demos, and unsupported claims.

groups.planning_use: Use this when deciding whether the core workflow is ready for broader testing.
groups.purpose: Shows whether the build does the thing it claims to do.

groups.planning_use: Use this after correctness is credible, or when field constraints are tight.
groups.purpose: Shows whether the build does the work fast enough and cheaply enough.

groups.planning_use: Use this before calling an improvement durable or release-ready.
groups.purpose: Shows whether good results repeat instead of appearing once.

groups.planning_use: Use this to decide what to focus on, stop doing, connect, or delegate.
groups.purpose: Shows whether the work loop is producing useful evidence or wasting time.

how_to_use_for_planning: Start by collecting one accepted real report; until then BETA is planning from project context, not proving improvement.
Pick one project goal or claim.
Choose the metric that would prove movement toward that goal.
Collect one real baseline report or source input.
Make one focused change or run one focused bench/field/test cycle.
Repeat the same scenario and compare the metric to the baseline.
Use the Manager screen to turn the result into the next priority, risk, or stop-doing item.

metric_playbook.summary: BETA uses metrics for three jobs: prove the project works, prove it is improving, and decide what work deserves attention next.

decision_rules: If a critical metric regresses, stabilize it before adding new features.
If a claim is thin, collect missing evidence before advertising improvement.
If a metric improves, add a regression guard before moving on.
If effort looks wasted, verify with issue, CI, or time-log data before changing priorities.

later.evidence_to_collect: A fresh matching proof report plus before/after metric comparison.
steps: Run the same scenario used by the comparable baseline.
Capture report.json and import it into BETA.
Check whether the target metric improved, stabilized, or regressed again.
If it regresses again, profile the owning code path before adding new features.
later.stop_condition: Do not move this to done until the metric is stable or improving across repeated comparable runs.
later.success_metric: High-value AI recommendations have a decision, owner, and result metric.
later.why_now: AI advice should become tracked actions whose impact can be measured later.

data_to_connect.next_step: Import real bench, field, CI, hardware, or project proof reports with no demo/synthetic/local-proof markers.

data_to_connect.next_step: Replace quarantined reports with real evidence or mark real reports explicitly with metadata.data_authenticity=real.
data_to_connect.why: Synthetic, demo, smoke, fixture, and local proof reports stay visible for audit but are excluded from metrics.

data_to_connect.next_step: Repeat the same scenario after changes so every metric has a before/after comparison.
data_to_connect.why: A matching prior scenario separates real progress from one-off numbers.

data_to_connect.next_step: Run the same scenario several times before treating a change as proven.
data_to_connect.why: Multiple runs expose noise, flaky behavior, and repeated regressions.

data_to_connect.next_step: Add at least one scale or field-like scenario beside the current coastal proof.
data_to_connect.why: More scenarios reduce the risk of proving only one narrow demo path.

data_to_connect.next_step: Run proof scenarios at 100, 500, and 1000+ observations and compare the curves.
data_to_connect.why: Larger samples make performance, storage, and reliability claims harder to fake.

data_to_connect.next_step: Use auto-strong for serious reviews; keep deterministic metrics as the source of truth.
data_to_connect.why: Ollama reviews weak evidence, missing measurements, experiment ideas, and next actions.

data_to_connect.next_step: Register planned sources, then connect real files or folders as they become available.
data_to_connect.why: Source connectors define which project inputs BETA should expect for issues, CI, docs, bench, field, metrics, and AI sessions.

operating_metrics.why: Each tracked build needs a setup profile, goals, source paths, and project type.
Goals tell BETA what outcomes the metrics are supposed to support.
Connected sources make the manager brief factual instead of guessy.
Issues, CI, AI sessions, docs, bench logs, and field logs explain why metrics moved.
Snapshots show Git state, source/test/doc balance, CI presence, and project structure.
Source volume helps size the project and compare testing/documentation balance.
Test volume is an early signal for regression protection and QA maturity.
Dirty worktrees can make evidence hard to reproduce unless changes are explained.
Todos are the committed project-management plan: what is planned, doing, blocked, done, or dropped.
Open todos show the remaining committed work BETA should help steer.
Blocked todos explain why progress may stall even when evidence metrics are quiet.
Completion rate shows plan progress, but completed todos should include evidence.
Structured work logs show where effort went, what produced evidence, and what got blocked.
Higher means more time is tied to tests, reports, decisions, or validation evidence.
High values reveal time sinks that should be removed or made explicit risks.
Planned measurements convert claims into tests and acceptance evidence.
Risks mark the places most likely to waste time or weaken proof.

Keep active todos current and close done work with evidence.
Record minutes, category, outcome, evidence, blocker, and next step after meaningful work.
Capture AI decisions, discarded ideas, and tested recommendations.

planning_loop: Claim: write the thing you want to be able to say is true.
Metric: define the number, pass/fail, or evidence signal that would prove it.
Scenario: run the same test or validation path repeatedly.
Evidence: import the report, CI result, bench log, field note, or operator signoff.
Manager decision: focus, stop, protect, or connect a missing source.
project_setup.summary: Use this setup guide to turn BETA from a dashboard into a project manager: goals define intent, metrics define proof, and evidence changes the plan.

ai_collaboration_contract: Save important AI sessions or summaries as project inputs.

Do not count AI claims as proof unless a test or source supports them.
Track which AI recommendations were tried and whether later metrics improved.

Connect source, test, issue, CI, AI-work, and bench/field evidence where available.
Review the operating plan weekly and after every major run.
